content:
# Luke Chapter 7 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 93-95 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_007_page_001.jpg, LUK_007_page_002.jpg, LUK_007_page_003.jpg

---

**1** After he had completed all his words in the hearing of the people, he entered into Capernaum.

**2** And a certain centurion's servant, who was dear to him, was ill and about to die.

**3** And having heard about Jesus, he sent to him elders of the Jews, asking him that he might come and save his servant.

**4** And when they came to Jesus, they entreated him earnestly, saying, "He is worthy that you should grant this to him,

**5** for he loves our nation, and he himself built the synagogue for us."

**6** And Jesus went with them. And when he was now not far from the house, the centurion sent friends, saying to him, "Lord, do not trouble yourself, for I am not worthy that you should come under my roof.

**7** Therefore neither did I consider myself worthy to come to you. But speak a word, and let my servant be healed.

**8** For I also am a man set under authority, having soldiers under me, and I say to this one, 'Go,' and he goes; and to another, 'Come,' and he comes; and to my servant, 'Do this,' and he does it."

**9** And when Jesus heard these things, he marveled at him, and turning to the crowd following him, he said, "I say to you, not even in Israel have I found such faith."

**10** And those who had been sent, having returned to the house, found the servant in good health.

**11** And it came to pass on the next day that he went to a city called Nain, and his disciples went with him, and a great crowd.

**12** And as he drew near to the gate of the city, behold, a dead man was being carried out, the only son of his mother, and she was a widow; and a considerable crowd from the city was with her.

**13** And when the Lord saw her, he had compassion on her and said to her, "Do not weep."

**14** And having come forward, he touched the bier, and the bearers stood still. And he said, "Young man, I say to you, arise."

**15** And the dead man sat up and began to speak. And he gave him to his mother.

**16** And fear seized them all, and they glorified God, saying, "A great prophet has been raised up among us," and, "God has visited his people."

**17** And this report concerning him went out in all Judea and in all the surrounding region.

**18** And the disciples of John reported to him concerning all these things.

**19** And John, having summoned two of his disciples, sent them to the Lord, saying, "Are you the Coming One, or are we to look for another?"

**20** And when the men came to him, they said, "John the Baptist sent us to you, saying, 'Are you the Coming One, or are we to look for another?'"

**21** In that hour he healed many of diseases and afflictions and evil spirits, and to many who were blind he granted sight.

**22** And he answered and said to them, "Go and report to John what you have seen and heard: the blind receive sight, the lame walk, lepers are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, the poor have the good news proclaimed to them.

**23** And blessed is whoever is not offended on account of me."

**24** And when the messengers of John had departed, he began to speak to the crowds concerning John: "What did you go out into the wilderness to behold? A reed shaken by the wind?

**25** But what did you go out to see? A man clothed in soft garments? Behold, those who are in splendid clothing and luxury are in the palaces of kings.

**26** But what did you go out to see? A prophet? Yes, I say to you, and more than a prophet.

**27** This is he concerning whom it is written: 'Behold, I send my messenger before your face, who shall prepare your way before you.'

**28** I say to you, among those born of women there is no one greater than John. Yet the one who is least in the kingdom of God is greater than he."

**29** And all the people who heard, and the tax collectors, justified God, having been baptized with the baptism of John.

**30** But the Pharisees and the lawyers rejected the counsel of God for themselves, not having been baptized by him.

**31** "To what then shall I liken the men of this generation, and what are they like?

**32** They are like children sitting in the marketplace and calling to one another, saying, 'We played the flute for you, and you did not dance; we sang a dirge, and you did not weep.'

**33** For John the Baptist has come neither eating bread nor drinking wine, and you say, 'He has a demon.'

**34** The Son of Man has come eating and drinking, and you say, 'Behold, a man who is a glutton and a drunkard, a friend of tax collectors and sinners.'

**35** And wisdom is justified by all her children."

**36** And one of the Pharisees asked him to eat with him. And having entered into the house of the Pharisee, he reclined at table.

**37** And behold, a woman in the city who was a sinner, having learned that he was reclining at table in the house of the Pharisee, brought an alabaster flask of ointment,

**38** and standing behind at his feet, weeping, she began to wet his feet with her tears, and she wiped them with the hair of her head, and she kissed his feet and anointed them with the ointment.

**39** And when the Pharisee who had invited him saw this, he spoke within himself, saying, "If this man were a prophet, he would know who and what sort of woman this is who touches him, that she is a sinner."

**40** And Jesus answered and said to him, "Simon, I have something to say to you." And he said, "Teacher, speak."

**41** "A certain moneylender had two debtors. The one owed five hundred denarii, and the other fifty.

**42** When they were unable to repay, he forgave them both. Which of them therefore will love him more?"

**43** Simon answered and said, "I suppose the one to whom he forgave more." And he said to him, "You have judged rightly."

**44** And turning to the woman, he said to Simon, "Do you see this woman? I entered your house; you gave me no water for my feet, but she has wet my feet with her tears and wiped them with her hair.

**45** You gave me no kiss, but she, from the time I entered, has not ceased kissing my feet.

**46** You did not anoint my head with oil, but she has anointed my feet with ointment.

**47** Therefore I say to you, her sins, which are many, have been forgiven, for she loved much. But the one who is forgiven little, loves little."

**48** And he said to her, "Your sins have been forgiven."

**49** And those reclining at table with him began to say among themselves, "Who is this, who even forgives sins?"

**50** And he said to the woman, "Your faith has saved you. Go in peace."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations (Codex Sinaiticus)

1. **Page layout:** Luke 7 begins on page 93 (folio 47v), column 2, immediately following the conclusion of Luke 6:49. The chapter continues through page 94 (folio 48r) across all four columns and concludes on page 95 (folio 48v), column 1, where Luke 8 begins.

2. **Verse 2 — "dear to him" (ENTIMOC AUTW):** Sinaiticus reads ENTIMOC, meaning "valued, precious, dear." The centurion's servant is described not merely as useful but as personally cherished. The LOGOS renders "dear to him" to preserve the affective dimension of the Greek without over-interpreting the relationship.

3. **Verse 3 — "elders of the Jews" (PRESBUTEROUS TWN IOUDAIWN):** Sinaiticus preserves this delegation of Jewish elders as intermediaries. Luke alone among the evangelists includes this detail (cf. Matt 8:5, where the centurion comes directly). The LOGOS follows Sinaiticus without harmonizing with Matthew.

4. **Verse 7 — "speak a word" (EIPE LOGW):** Sinaiticus reads EIPE LOGW, literally "speak by a word." The centurion recognizes that Jesus's authority operates through speech alone, without physical presence. The LOGOS renders "speak a word" to preserve the instrumental dative.

5. **Verse 9 — "not even in Israel" (OUDE EN TW ISRAHL):** Sinaiticus reads OUDE EN TW ISRAHL. This is a remarkable declaration: a Gentile military officer has surpassed the faith of the covenant people. The LOGOS preserves the emphatic negation OUDE ("not even").

6. **Verse 11 — "on the next day" (EN TH EXHC):** Sinaiticus reads EN TH EXHC, providing a close temporal link between the Capernaum healing and the Nain miracle. Some manuscripts read EN TW EXHC or omit the temporal marker. The LOGOS follows Sinaiticus.

7. **Verse 11 — "Nain" (NAIN):** A small village in Galilee, identified with modern Nein at the foot of the Hill of Moreh. This is the only mention of Nain in the New Testament. The LOGOS retains the traditional transliteration.

8. **Verse 13 — "the Lord" (O KURIOC):** Sinaiticus reads O KURIOC here rather than IHSOUC. Luke's use of the title "the Lord" as a narrative designation for Jesus is characteristic of his Gospel and reflects the post-resurrection perspective of the evangelist. The LOGOS preserves the title as it appears in the manuscript.

9. **Verse 14 — "the bier" (THC SOROU):** SOROC denotes the open funeral bier or coffin on which the body was carried. The LOGOS renders "the bier" as a formal equivalent, preserving the funerary context without anachronism.

10. **Verse 16 — "visited" (EPESKEYATO):** Sinaiticus reads EPESKEYATO, from EPISKEPTOMAI, meaning "to look upon with care, to visit." This is a theologically loaded term in Luke, echoing the language of divine visitation in the Old Testament (cf. Exod 4:31; Ruth 1:6; Luke 1:68, 78). The LOGOS preserves "visited" as the formal equivalent.

11. **Verse 19 — "the Coming One" (O ERXOMENOC):** The participle O ERXOMENOC functions as a messianic title, rooted in Ps 118:26 and Mal 3:1. The LOGOS capitalizes "Coming One" to mark its titular function, distinguishing it from ordinary usage of the verb ERXOMAI.

12. **Verse 22 — Isaianic allusions:** Jesus's response alludes to multiple passages from Isaiah (Isa 29:18-19; 35:5-6; 61:1). Sinaiticus preserves the list without variation. The LOGOS translates each element as a present-tense declaration, reflecting the Greek present indicative: "the blind receive sight, the lame walk."

13. **Verse 27 — Malachi citation (Mal 3:1):** Sinaiticus reads the quotation in the form: IDOU APOSTELLW TON AGGELON MOU PRO PROSWPOU SOU OC KATASKEUASEI THN ODON SOU EMPROSQEN SOU. The LOGOS renders "before your face" for PRO PROSWPOU SOU, preserving the Hebraic idiom.

14. **Verse 28 — "there is no one greater than John":** Sinaiticus reads MEIZON EN GENNHTOIC GUNAIKWN IWANOU OUDEIC ESTIN. Some manuscripts add PROFHTHC ("prophet") before IWANOU. Sinaiticus omits it. The LOGOS follows the shorter reading of Sinaiticus.

15. **Verse 29 — "justified God" (EDIKAIWSAN TON QEON):** The verb DIKAIOW here means "to declare righteous, to vindicate." The people and tax collectors acknowledged God's righteous purpose by submitting to John's baptism. The LOGOS renders "justified God" as a formal equivalent of this unusual theological expression.

16. **Verse 30 — "lawyers" (NOMIKOI):** Sinaiticus reads NOMIKOI, a term distinct from GRAMMATEIC ("scribes"). NOMIKOI denotes experts in the Mosaic law, legal scholars. The LOGOS renders "lawyers" to distinguish them from the scribes, following Luke's own lexical distinction.

17. **Verse 35 — "wisdom is justified by all her children" (EDIKAIWQH H SOFIA APO TWN TEKNWN AUTHS PANTWN):** Sinaiticus reads TEKNWN ("children") rather than ERGWN ("works," Matt 11:19). The LOGOS follows Sinaiticus. The personification of Wisdom as a mother whose children vindicate her echoes Jewish Wisdom tradition.

18. **Verse 37 — "alabaster flask of ointment" (ALABASTRON MUROU):** ALABASTRON denotes a sealed flask, typically of alabaster stone, used for storing costly perfumed ointment. MURON is aromatic ointment or perfumed oil. The LOGOS renders both terms to preserve the material specificity of the scene.

19. **Verse 38 — "wiped them with the hair of her head" (TAIC QRIXIN THC KEFALHC AUTHS EXEMASSEN):** Sinaiticus reads THC KEFALHC AUTHS ("of her head"), specifying explicitly. The loosening of a woman's hair in public was a mark of profound social transgression, intensifying the scandalous nature of her act. The LOGOS preserves this detail.

20. **Verse 41 — "denarii" (DHNARIA):** The LOGOS retains the Greek monetary term as a transliteration. A denarius was approximately one day's wage for a laborer. Five hundred denarii thus represented well over a year's earnings, while fifty denarii was roughly two months' wages. The disproportion drives the parable's logic.

21. **Verse 47 — "for she loved much" (OTI HGAPHSEN POLU):** The OTI clause is causal, but its logical direction is debated: does the love cause the forgiveness, or does the forgiveness cause the love? The parable in vv. 41-43 establishes that forgiveness precedes love. The LOGOS renders "for she loved much" as a formal equivalent, preserving the Greek syntax. The following clause — "the one who is forgiven little, loves little" — confirms that forgiveness is the ground and love the consequence.

22. **Verse 50 — "Go in peace" (POREUOU EIC EIRHNHN):** The phrase EIC EIRHNHN ("into peace") differs from EN EIRHHNH ("in peace"). The preposition EIC with the accusative suggests movement toward a state of peace, a new condition being entered. The LOGOS renders "Go in peace" as the dignified formal equivalent, following established English liturgical usage.

### Translation Philosophy

- **"Centurion" (EKATONTARXHC):** Retained as the standard transliteration (vv. 2, 6) rather than "captain of a hundred." The Greek term is itself a calque of the Latin centurio, denoting an officer commanding approximately eighty to one hundred soldiers. The transliterated form is universally recognized in English.
- **"Tax collectors" (TELWNAI):** Rendered "tax collectors" (vv. 29, 34) rather than "publicans" (KJV). TELWNHC denotes a collector of tolls and indirect taxes, a figure of social contempt due to collaboration with Rome and routine overcharging. "Tax collectors" communicates the occupation without archaism.
- **"Moneylender" (DANISTHC):** Rendered "moneylender" (v. 41) rather than "creditor." DANISTHC specifically denotes one who lends at interest. "Moneylender" preserves the commercial specificity of the parable's setting.
- **"Reclined at table" (KATEKLIQH / KATAKEIMENON):** Rendered "reclined at table" (vv. 36-37) rather than "sat down to eat." Formal dining in the Greco-Roman world took place on couches arranged around a central table. The posture explains how the woman could approach Jesus's feet from behind (v. 38). The LOGOS preserves this cultural detail.
- **"Ointment" (MURON):** Rendered "ointment" (vv. 37-38, 46) rather than "perfume." MURON denotes an aromatic preparation, typically oil-based, used for anointing the body. "Ointment" preserves the tactile, ritual dimension of the substance, distinguishing it from modern fragrance.
- **"Forgiven" (AFEWNTAI / AFEQHSAN):** The perfect tense AFEWNTAI in v. 47 ("have been forgiven") and the present AFEWNTAI in v. 48 ("have been forgiven") are rendered consistently to reflect the completed-yet-enduring state of divine pardon. The LOGOS preserves the passive voice, indicating God as the implied agent of forgiveness.
